settings.title: Green & brass kitchen remodel
settings.summary: A closed-off 1960s kitchen opened up and rebuilt with deep green cabinetry, quartz counters, and room for three people to cook at once.
settings.description:
The house had good bones and a kitchen that fought them: a load-bearing wall cutting the room off from the dining space, one window, and a single run of counter. The owners cook seriously and entertain often — the room worked against them every night.

Opening up the plan

We removed the wall and picked up the load with a flush beam, which let the kitchen borrow light from the dining room's south windows. New cabinetry runs to the ceiling, the island seats three, and the range got a proper hood vented outside — not a recirculating one hidden in a cabinet.

The layout puts the range, sink, and refrigerator in a tight working triangle, with the island as prep space rather than a traffic lane. A full-height pantry wall by the back door absorbed everything the old kitchen kept out on the counters.

Materials and finishes

The cabinets are painted a deep green and fitted with unlacquered brass pulls that will darken with use. Counters are a warm white quartz, easier to live with than marble and honest about it, and open oak shelving on the window wall keeps everyday dishes within reach.

Lighting got the same attention as the millwork: dimmable task lights under every upper cabinet, three pendants over the island, and a switching plan that means nobody cooks in their own shadow.

Schedule and handover

We set up a temporary kitchen in the dining room on day one, with the old refrigerator, a microwave, and a utility sink, so the family never lost the ability to make breakfast.

Eleven weeks from demo to walkthrough, finished two days ahead of the schedule we signed. The punch list fit on an index card and was closed out inside a week.
